content.text:
## Role

- You are a subagent implementing exactly one issue in a larger plan. Stay strictly within its scope.

## Work item context

## Orientation (why this issue exists — not work to do)
- Project goal: Migrate integrations-core off the requests library onto httpx via an HTTP-agnostic protocol and shared exception library.
- Issue phase/group: Phase 3 — Repo agnosticization (vertical slices) (HTTPX-G3)
- Phase description: **Goal:** remove every `requests`-specific reference from shipped **integration code and its tests** (~50 integrations), leaving each integration on the agnostic surface only. Backend stays `requests`.

**Decomposition — vertical slices, not horizontal layers.** Following PR #24310 (base translation + integration narrowing + those integrations' test updates in one unit), each issue owns a **batch of integrations and fixes all of their `requests` references at once** — production catch-narrowing, session/auth/factory adoption, and the paired test decoupling, plus per-integration `changelog.d/<PR>.fixed`. This keeps every issue self-contained and green, matches the shipped workflow, and avoids R2 serializing a separate "production" and "test" issue that touch the same integration's files.

**Per-slice moves (each integration takes the subset that applies):**
- Narrow `self.http` catch sites to agnostic-only (~47 sites; no dual-catch). Swap bare `raise requests...` (nifi); reparent elastic `AuthenticationError`; drop the cloud_foundry_api dual-arm.
- Replace `self.http.session.*`/`_session` accessors (~5: cisco_aci, http_check, avi_vantage) with the Phase 2 capabilities.
- Migrate `AuthBase` subclasses (voltdb) to the agnostic auth hook.
- Reroute bespoke `RequestsWrapper(...)` (kube_*, hpe_aruba_edgeconnect) through the Phase 2 factory.
- Route bare `requests.get/post` (legacy openstack) through the protocol.
- In the same slice: move that integration's `requests.Session` test patches to `mock_http`/OM fixtures, replace test-built `requests.Response` with `MockHTTPResponse`, and swap `requests.exceptions` test imports/asserts to agnostic.
- One cross-cutting slice: retire the `datadog_checks_dev` `MockResponse` and migrate its consumers (argocd, bentoml, kubernetes_state, marklogic, prefect, sonatype_nexus, traefik_mesh, vsphere, +base ref) together.

**Batching:** group by mechanical class / subsystem / CODEOWNERS so each batch is reviewable and collision-free; slices are independent and run in parallel once their Phase 2 dependency (if any) is met. The catch-narrowing slices carry the exception-translation dependency; slices touching only session/auth/factory carry the matching capability dependency. Harness HTTP (dd_environment, WaitFor, fixture-seeding, ~28 integrations) is **exempt** — the grep gate is interception-only. Assert observable behavior only; never call counts/URLs. Any latent bug found is split out to master separately (per the "Changes to Split Out" page), not fixed here.

**Validation (high level):** each slice's integrations pass `ddev test`, and a committed grep asserts those integrations carry no `requests`-specific reference in production or interception-path tests.

Work item: HTTPX-10 — Reroute kube_* and hpe_aruba_edgeconnect through the base factory

## Intent
Migrate the checks that build a bespoke RequestsWrapper directly onto the Phase 2 factory seam and narrow their catches, so their HTTP client is constructed through the one swappable point instead of hard-wiring the requests-backed wrapper.

## Description
Depends on the Phase 2 factory seam (HTTPX-3) **and** the Phase 2 session capabilities (HTTPX-2). Owned integrations: **kube_scheduler, kube_proxy, kube_metrics_server, kube_dns, kube_controller_manager, hpe_aruba_edgeconnect** (kubelet is handled in batch B).

Reroute each bespoke `RequestsWrapper(...)` construction through the Phase 2 overridable factory (default behavior unchanged), narrow `self.http` catches to `http_exceptions.*`, and remove `requests` imports. Fix these integrations' interception-path tests onto the factory/`mock_http` in the same slice. Add `<integration>/changelog.d/22676.fixed`. The gate additionally forbids direct `RequestsWrapper(` construction in these packages.

**hpe_aruba_edgeconnect specifics** (found in the HTTPX-1 census / HTTPX-2 review): it reaches into the session directly — `self.http.session.cookies.get(...)` and `self.http.session.headers.update({...})`. Migrate these onto the agnostic surface: use the Phase 2 `get_cookie(name, default=None)` capability for the cookie read, and replace `session.headers.update({...})` with per-key `set_header(name, value)` calls (the protocol exposes single-header set, which is equivalent to a dict update). Do NOT reopen the now-frozen protocol to add a bulk-header method unless a call site genuinely cannot be expressed as repeated `set_header` — flag it to the Orchestrator first if so, since it would ripple into HTTPX-15 (httpx2 parity).

Assert observable behavior only; split latent bugs to master.

## Acceptance criteria
- No `requests` reference and no direct `RequestsWrapper(` construction remains in these integrations' `datadog_checks/` packages; files compile.
- The wrapper is obtained through the Phase 2 factory.
- Unit tests pass under `ddev test <integration>`.
- `22676.fixed` entries added.
(Your work must satisfy the acceptance criteria above.)

## Context from completed dependencies
- HTTPX-1: All acceptance criteria met. Done.

## Completion summary

### What I changed
Added two committed files (both under `.r2/`, currently untracked so R2 will commit them):

- **`.r2/httpx_census.py`** — a reproducible census tool that:
  - Scans every git-tracked `*.py` via `git ls-files '*.py'`, excluding the base wrapper `datadog_checks_base/datadog_checks/base/utils/http.py` entirely.
  - Classifies each non-comment code line into 7 categories via regex: `except_requests_exceptions`, `import_requests`, `requests_attr`, `http_session_access`, `authbase_subclass`, `requests_response_construction`, `requests_session_patch`.
  - Tags each file with `integration` (top-level path component), `shipped` (False for `datadog_checks_base/`, `datadog_checks_dev/`, `ddev/`, and any top-level dotdir including `.ddev/`; True otherwise), and `test` (path contains `/tests/`, or filename starts with `test_`, or is `conftest.py`).
  - Enforces **translation totality** as a static check before writing anything: greps the base wrapper for `except requests_exceptions.RequestException`. If absent, prints `TRANSLATION GAP` to stderr and exits 1.
  - Writes the snapshot and prints total/shipped ref-file counts and shipped-integration count.
- **`.r2/httpx_census.json`** — the generated snapshot, keyed by file, each entry having `integration`/`shipped`/`test`/`hits` (each hit carries `category`/`line`/`text`). Written with `sort_keys=True` for deterministic output.

### How I verified it
- `python3 .r2/httpx_census.py` exits 0; reports **239 total** ref-files, **200 shipped**, **98 shipped integrations touched**, and confirms the wrapper catches `RequestException`.
- Reran the script and byte-compared the JSON: identical (deterministic).
- Negative path: temporarily changed the wrapper's `except requests_exceptions.RequestException` to `HTTPError`; the script printed `TRANSLATION GAP` and exited 1. Restored the wrapper (`git diff` on it is now empty).
- Reconciled the RFC corrections against the snapshot:
  - Only `voltdb/datadog_checks/voltdb/client.py` matches `authbase_subclass` (single hit repo-wide).
  - `nifi/datadog_checks/nifi/api.py` carries a bare `from requests.exceptions import HTTPError` (tagged `import_requests`).
  - `cloud_foundry_api/.../cloud_foundry_api.py` is present, shipped, importing both requests and `datadog_checks.base.utils.http_exceptions` (dual-arm).
- Spot-checked schema: base wrapper excluded from JSON; a shipped test file (`activemq/tests/conftest.py`) tagged `shipped=True, test=True`; an exempt file (`.ddev/...`) tagged `shipped=False`.

### Things I noticed (not acted on, out of scope)
- Category totals: `requests_attr` 790, `requests_session_patch` 180, `import_requests` 156, `except_requests_exceptions` 57, `http_session_access` 9, `requests_response_construction` 1, `authbase_subclass` 1. The single `requests_response_construction` and single `authbase_subclass` hits are notably low; downstream sweeps may want to confirm test-built `requests.Response` objects aren't being constructed via other idioms (e.g. `mock.Mock(spec=Response)` or `Response()` after a bare import) that this narrow `requests.Response(` regex won't catch.
- Comment filtering only skips full-comment lines (`lstrip().startswith('#')`); inline trailing comments and matches inside string literals are not stripped, so a small number of hits may be non-code references. This is consistent with the issue's "regex over non-comment lines" wording but is a known source of minor over-counting.
- `nifi` also has the same bare `HTTPError` import in `nifi/tests/test_unit.py`, not just `api.py`.
- I did not add a changelog entry: `.r2/` tooling does not ship with the Agent and is not `ddev`/`datadog_checks_dev`, so per the repo AGENTS.md changelog rules it is out of scope. No integration code was modified.
- HTTPX-2: Done. The `close()` comment in `http_protocol.py` now uses "idempotent" with a short parenthetical clarification. Validation passes (166 passed, 1 skipped); `git status` clean.
- HTTPX-3: Done. Addressed the review comment.

Changes made:
- `http_protocol.py`: shortened the `OutgoingRequest` and `HTTPRequestAuth` comments to plain short sentences, removed backticks (nothing renders markdown there), removed semicolons.
- `checks/base.py`: removed the semicolon and backticks in the `create_http_wrapper` docstring.
- `utils/http.py`: removed backticks in the `_RequestsAuthHookAdapter` docstring.

No em-dashes were present. Lint passes; the auth-hook, factory-seam, and capabilities tests pass (20 passed). The two `# noqa: G` warnings are pre-existing in `create_socket_connection`, unrelated to this work.

## Scope rules

- Implement only this issue — nothing else. Do not start other issues, fix unrelated problems you notice, or improvise your own requirements, product behavior, or follow-up work beyond what this issue requires.
- The orientation context (project goal, phase) is only to explain WHY this issue exists; never widen your work to it.
- R2 commits your worktree for you once you finish, so avoid committing the changes you make for this issue. Read-only git (`git status`, `git diff`, `git log`) is fine if you need it.

## Correct-by-default work

- Correctness is the floor, not the goal; aim for the change that is correct for the codebase, callers, and users.
- Handle edge cases and error paths implied by the acceptance criteria, current call paths, data contracts, and surrounding code.
- Match existing conventions for style, architecture, tests, naming, errors, logging, and user-facing behavior.
- Stay consistent with callers and dependents: preserve contracts they rely on, update them when this issue requires it, and avoid surprising downstream behavior.
- If acceptance criteria are wrong, contradictory, or impossible, do your best within this issue's scope and call it out plainly in the completion summary.
- If you are truly blocked by missing prerequisites, contradictory requirements, or something outside this worktree, call request_human with a concise explanation instead of guessing.

## Progress narration

- At natural milestones (starting a distinct sub-task, finishing a significant step), emit a short progress note — one or two lines — as normal visible output text so the run is followable.
- This is NOT internal reasoning/"thinking" (thinking is recorded only as activity and not posted as a message), NOT a tool call, and NOT ask_worker_question. Plain assistant text is surfaced by R2 as a live worker post without changing issue status.
- Keep narration milestone-bounded: milestones only, not a play-by-play of every small command, result, or observation.

## Communication and questions

- Use ask_worker_question only for a decision that is consequential AND cannot be reasonably resolved from the issue + the codebase. It is not for routine progress; otherwise, decide sensibly and note the assumption in the completion summary.
- Asking opens a visible comment thread and parks the issue in `needs_attention`; the run ends while the issue waits for a human/Orchestrator reply, then this same session is resumed with the answer. Before stopping, finish any other in-scope work not blocked by the answer.
- Keep the channels distinct: ask_worker_question parks the issue for a durable answer (resumed later); request_human is a hard abort/escalation when you cannot continue or complete the work correctly; progress narration and the end-of-run completion summary never change issue status.

## Completion summary

- When you finish, end with a DETAILED, SELF-CONTAINED completion summary that stands on its own without the codebase in front of the reader: what you changed (files and behavior), how you verified it, and — plainly — anything you NOTICED that may matter elsewhere (out-of-scope work, a latent problem, an impact on other parts of the code).
- Describe what you observed; do NOT classify, rank, or triage it. The Orchestrator holds the full plan and will judge whether any of it warrants follow-up work.

content.thinking:
I'm seeing the core tension: `create_http_wrapper()` pulls from `self.instance`, but the Kubernetes-specific checks build their own wrappers from a custom config dict instead. The challenge is routing all those bespoke `RequestsWrapper` constructions through the factory while keeping the default behavior intact.

Let me look at how PR #24310 handled this pattern in other integrations—specifically how they managed per-endpoint or per-check custom configs through the factory seam. The constraint is clear: I can't reopen the frozen protocol, so `create_http_wrapper` stays no-arg, but I need a way to pass custom configs through it without breaking that contract.

Actually, I realize `create_http_wrapper` lives on AgentCheck in base.py, not on the frozen protocol itself. So I could extend its signature to accept optional overrides like `instance`, `init_config`, or `remapper` without violating the frozen surface. Let me check what HTTPX-3's commit intended here and whether adding parameters to the factory was part of the design.

from typing import Any

__all__ = [
    'HTTPError',
    'HTTPRequestError',
    'HTTPStatusError',
    'HTTPTimeoutError',
    'HTTPConnectionError',
    'HTTPInvalidURLError',
    'HTTPSSLError',
]


class HTTPError(Exception):
    def __init__(self, message: str, response: Any = None, request: Any = None):
        super().__init__(message)
        self.response = response
        self.request = request


class HTTPRequestError(HTTPError):
    pass


class HTTPStatusError(HTTPError):
    pass


class HTTPTimeoutError(HTTPRequestError):
    pass


class HTTPConnectionError(HTTPRequestError):
    pass


class HTTPInvalidURLError(HTTPRequestError):
    pass


class HTTPSSLError(HTTPConnectionError):

# (C) Datadog, Inc. 2026-present

from __future__ import annotations

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from collections.abc import Mapping, MutableMapping
from typing import Any, Iterator, Protocol

# Frozen backend-neutral HTTP surface. RequestsWrapper implements it on requests today; a future
# HTTPX2Wrapper implements the same surface on httpx. Do not change existing methods, attributes,
# or their semantics without coordinating both backends. Capabilities expose behavior, never a
# backend object (no requests or httpx type is returned).


class HTTPResponseProtocol(Protocol):
    status_code: int
    content: bytes
    text: str
    headers: Mapping[str, str]

    @property
    def ok(self) -> bool: ...
    @property
    def reason(self) -> str: ...

    def json(self, **kwargs: Any) -> Any: ...
    def raise_for_status(self) -> None: ...
    def close(self) -> None: ...
    def iter_content(self, chunk_size: int | None = None, decode_unicode: bool = False) -> Iterator[bytes | str]: ...
    def iter_lines(
        self,
        chunk_size: int | None = None,
        decode_unicode: bool = False,
        delimiter: bytes | str | None = None,
    ) -> Iterator[bytes | str]: ...
    def __enter__(self) -> HTTPResponseProtocol: ...
    def __exit__(self, exc_type: Any, exc_val: Any, exc_tb: Any) -> bool | None: ...
    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[bytes | str]: ...


class OutgoingRequest(Protocol):
    # Mutable view of an outgoing request handed to an auth hook. A hook adds entries to headers
    # or params. Each backend builds its own instance and applies the values onto its native
    # request.
    url: str
    headers: MutableMapping[str, str]
    params: MutableMapping[str, str]


class HTTPRequestAuth(ABC):
    # Backend-neutral auth hook that contributes headers or params to an outgoing request.
    # RequestsWrapper adapts it to requests.auth.AuthBase today. A future HTTPX2Wrapper adapts it
    # to httpx.Auth. Reactive challenge-response schemes (digest, kerberos, ntlm) stay in the
    # auth_type dispatch, not here.
    @abstractmethod
    def __call__(self, request: OutgoingRequest) -> None:
        """Contribute headers or params to the outgoing request in place."""


class HTTPClientProtocol(Protocol):
    options: dict[str, Any]

    # Whether the client trusts environment config (proxies, auth, CA bundles).
    trust_env: bool

    def get(self, url: str, **options: Any) -> HTTPResponseProtocol: ...
    def post(self, url: str, **options: Any) -> HTTPResponseProtocol: ...
    def head(self, url: str, **options: Any) -> HTTPResponseProtocol: ...
    def put(self, url: str, **options: Any) -> HTTPResponseProtocol: ...
    def patch(self, url: str, **options: Any) -> HTTPResponseProtocol: ...
    def delete(self, url: str, **options: Any) -> HTTPResponseProtocol: ...
    def options_method(self, url: str, **options: Any) -> HTTPResponseProtocol: ...
    def get_header(self, name: str, default: str | None = None) -> str | None: ...
    def set_header(self, name: str, value: str) -> None: ...

    # Close any open connections. Idempotent (safe to call repeatedly or before any connection was
    # opened); the client stays usable and reconnects on the next request.
    def close(self) -> None: ...

    # Look up a persisted cookie by name, returning its value (or default) as a plain string.
    def get_cookie(self, name: str, default: str | None = None) -> str | None: ...

import pytest

from datadog_checks.base.utils.http_testing import MockHTTPResponse
from datadog_checks.kube_scheduler import KubeSchedulerCheck

from .common import make_mock_metrics

# Constants
CHECK_NAME = 'kube_scheduler'


@pytest.fixture()
def mock_metrics(mock_openmetrics_http):
    return make_mock_metrics(mock_openmetrics_http, 'metrics_slis_1.27.3.txt')


def test_check_metrics_slis(aggregator, mock_metrics, instance, mock_healthcheck_wrapper):
    c = KubeSchedulerCheck(CHECK_NAME, {}, [instance])
    c.check(instance)

    def assert_metric(name, **kwargs):
        # Wrapper to keep assertions < 120 chars
        aggregator.assert_metric("{}.{}".format(CHECK_NAME, name), **kwargs)

    assert_metric('slis.kubernetes_healthcheck', value=1, tags=['sli_name:ping'])
    assert_metric(
        'slis.kubernetes_healthchecks_total',
        value=2450,
        tags=['sli_name:ping', 'status:success'],
    )

    aggregator.assert_all_metrics_covered()


def test_check_metrics_slis_transform(aggregator, mock_metrics, instance, mock_healthcheck_wrapper):
    c = KubeSchedulerCheck(CHECK_NAME, {}, [instance])
    c.check(instance)

    def assert_metric(name, **kwargs):
        # Wrapper to keep assertions < 120 chars
        aggregator.assert_metric("{}.{}".format(CHECK_NAME, name), **kwargs)

    # Check that no metrics with `name` tag come through
    assert_metric('slis.kubernetes_healthcheck', count=0, metric_type=aggregator.GAUGE, tags=['name:attachdetach'])
    assert_metric(
        'slis.kubernetes_healthchecks_total',
        metric_type=aggregator.MONOTONIC_COUNT,
        count=0,
        tags=['name:attachdetach', 'status:success'],
    )


def test_check_metrics_slis_filter_by_type(aggregator, mock_metrics, instance, mock_healthcheck_wrapper):
    c = KubeSchedulerCheck(CHECK_NAME, {}, [instance])
    c.check(instance)

    def assert_metric(name, **kwargs):
        # Wrapper to keep assertions < 120 chars
        aggregator.assert_metric("{}.{}".format(CHECK_NAME, name), **kwargs)

    # Check that metrics with type other than `healthz` are filtered out
    assert_metric(
        'slis.kubernetes_healthcheck', count=0, metric_type=aggregator.GAUGE, tags=['sli_name:etcd', 'type:readyz']
    )

    assert_metric(
        'slis.kubernetes_healthchecks_total',
        metric_type=aggregator.MONOTONIC_COUNT,
        count=0,
        tags=['sli_name:etcd', 'status:success', 'type:readyz'],
    )


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    'status_code, expected_available',
    [(200, True), (404, False), (403, False)],
    ids=['200_available', '404_unavailable', '403_unavailable'],
)
def test_detect_sli_endpoint(mock_openmetrics_http, instance, status_code, expected_available):
    mock_openmetrics_http.get.return_value = MockHTTPResponse(status_code=status_code)
    c = KubeSchedulerCheck(CHECK_NAME, {}, [instance])
    assert c._slis_available is expected_available

## Completion summary

Rerouted `kube_scheduler`, `kube_proxy`, `kube_metrics_server`, `kube_dns`, `kube_controller_manager`, and `hpe_aruba_edgeconnect` off direct `RequestsWrapper` construction onto the Phase 2 factory, narrowed their catches, and migrated hpe_aruba's session access onto the agnostic surface.

### What I changed

**Base factory seam (`datadog_checks_base/datadog_checks/base/checks/base.py`)**
- `create_http_wrapper` gained an optional `instance: dict | None = None` param. When `None` it falls back to `self.instance or {}` (default behavior unchanged); otherwise it builds a wrapper for the passed config using the check's own `init_config`/`HTTP_CONFIG_REMAPPER`/`log`. This is what lets the per-endpoint kube handlers route through the factory without constructing `RequestsWrapper` in the integration packages. I chose the base param over the reviewer's "override returning a bespoke RequestsWrapper" suggestion because the acceptance criterion forbids any `RequestsWrapper(` construction in these packages; an override still constructs it there.
- Added `test_factory_instance_override_builds_for_given_config` to `datadog_checks_base/tests/base/utils/http/test_http.py`.

**5 kube_* checks** (`<int>/datadog_checks/<int>/<int>.py`) — identical pattern each:
- Removed `import requests` and `from datadog_checks.base.utils.http import RequestsWrapper`; added `from datadog_checks.base.utils.http_exceptions import HTTPError`.
- `except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:` → `except HTTPError as e:` (RequestException is the broadest requests base; HTTPError is the agnostic top-level, matching prior batch migrations).
- `RequestsWrapper(config, self.init_config, self.HTTP_CONFIG_REMAPPER, self.log)` → `self.create_http_wrapper(instance=config)`.

**hpe_aruba_edgeconnect**
- `check.py`: dropped the `RequestsWrapper` import; `_create_appliance_client` now uses `self.create_http_wrapper()` (its bespoke build was identical to the factory default).
- `client.py`: moved type-only imports under `TYPE_CHECKING` (`HTTPClientProtocol`, `HTTPResponseProtocol`), removed `from requests import Response` and the `RequestsWrapper` import; retyped `http: RequestsWrapper` → `HTTPClientProtocol` and `-> Response` → `HTTPResponseProtocol`. Session access replaced: `self._http.session.cookies.get(name)` → `self._http.get_cookie(name)`; `self._http.session.headers.update({k: v})` → `self._http.set_header(k, v)` (all single-key updates, no bulk-header need).

**Tests**
- 5 kube conftests: `mock_healthcheck_wrapper` now patches `<Check>.create_http_wrapper` instead of the removed `RequestsWrapper` symbol.
- 5 kube test files: dropped `import requests`, added the agnostic `HTTPError`, and changed the service-check `side_effect` from `requests.HTTPError('health check failed')` to `HTTPError('health check failed')` (agnostic instance with message).
- `hpe_aruba_edgeconnect/tests/test_unit.py`: the three login tests and the 401-retry test no longer poke `http.session.*`; added a `build_login_http(cookies)` helper (no leading underscore per repo rule) whose `get_cookie`/`set_header` back real dicts, and assertions now read the resulting `http.headers` dict — observable state, no call-count/URL assertions.

**Changelogs**: `22676.fixed` added for all six integrations.

### How I verified it
- Unit tests pass: kube_scheduler (13 passed, 1 skipped), kube_proxy (8/1), kube_metrics_server (3/1), kube_dns (3/1), kube_controller_manager (10/1), hpe_aruba_edgeconnect (51 passed via `-m unit`). Base factory tests: 4 passed.
- Grep gate clean: no `import requests`/`from requests`/`requests.`/`RequestsWrapper(`/`.session` in any of the six `datadog_checks/` packages or their tests.
- Lint passes for all six integrations and `datadog_checks_base` (only pre-existing `# noqa` warnings in unrelated files). Fixed one auto-format wrap in `kube_metrics_server/tests/conftest.py`.
- `py_compile` OK on all changed production files.

### Things I noticed (not acted on)
- `check.py` sets `self.http.persist_connections = True` and `http.persist_connections = True`; `persist_connections` is a RequestsWrapper attribute not declared on `HTTPClientProtocol`. This is pre-existing (self.http is protocol-typed already) and is not a `requests` token, so left as-is; it may need attention when a non-requests backend lands.
- The kube integrations' tests carry no `unit` pytest marker, so `ddev test <int> -- -m unit` deselects everything; I ran the full (mock-only, no docker) suite per integration instead. hpe_aruba does have `pytestmark = pytest.mark.unit`.
- The base factory signature change is a shared surface change (in `datadog_checks_base`); it is backward compatible (no-arg callers and no-arg subclass overrides unaffected), but any other issue touching `create_http_wrapper` should be aware of the new optional param.
- `hpe_aruba_edgeconnect/tests/test_unit.py::test_request_retries_once_on_401` retains its pre-existing `call_count`/URL assertions; I did not rewrite those since they are out of scope and not `requests` references.
